title: Lambda
text:
1 Introduction
In 2017, BoE Governor Mark Carney gave a speech titled ‘Lambda’ describing (i) how BoE interest rate policy had been confronted with a trade-off between returning inflation to the inflation target and stabilising output and (ii) how the Bank’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) confronted that trade-off.
In this note, I replicate and update parts of Governor Carney’s analysis. The period since Governor Carney’s 2017 speech has been marked by further episodes that imply a macro trade-off between reaching the inflation target and output stabilisation. The trade-off owes above all to factors that tend to raise inflation, while also opening up an output ‘gap’.


2 The macro-data: BoE forecasts of Inflation and Output gaps
Every three months since being made independent of the Government in 1997, the Bank of England has published forecasts for how it expects the UK economy to evolve. These forecasts are published as part of the Bank’s Monetary Policy Report (previously, Inflation Report). There has been only one exception since 1997 – in May 2020, at the height of the Covid-19 pandemic.
The plots below show the succession of these BoE forecasts – for CPI inflation, the level of GDP and the unemployment rate. The charts can be used to constructive a narrative of the various factors that shaped UK economic performance through this period, as well as the gradual process of learning their impact – as perceived by the Bank of England.


Reading 42 sheets 









In order to focus on the UK’s more recent experience, it is helpful to ‘zoom-in’ on the most recent macro forecasts, as shown below.









How are output gaps and inflation related to one another? The charts below give an indication, distinguishing three different phases:
- pre-Global Financial Crisis (GFC), when inflation was close to target and output varied little around its potential level so the output gap was small;
- Post-GFC and pre-Covid (2008-19), when inflation was close to target despite the estimated output gap varying significantly;
- Covid-19 and post-pandemic when inflation rose significantly above target, with significant variation in the output gap.
There were different macro phases in this era of inflation targeting. Pre-financial crisis (pre-GFC), inflation and output gap variability was low (first panel, below); in the post-GFC period and the post-pandemic period negative supply shocks dominated, leading to an inverse relation between inflation and the output gap (second panel, below); demand shocks, perhaps including looser fiscal policy, appeared to dominate during the pandemic period with a positive association between inflation and the output gap.









The Lambda of Governor Carney’s speech refers to the relative weight the policymaker, such as the BoE, places on output stabilisation relative to inflation stabilisation. So this captures the central bank’s preferences, in the face of certain macro shocks.
Governor Carney suggested a ‘lambda’ of 0.1 was an approximate lower bound to that relative weight attached to output stabilisation. The ‘cone’ of combinations of forecast CPI inflation and forecast output gap between values of lambda of 0.1 and 1.0 might represent something like a desired combination of inflation and output gap.











3 Conclusions
The most recent macro forecasts produced by the BoE placed the economy outside the cone shown in the chart above. That suggests more policy effort needed to push inflation upwards on a 2-year outlook for a given amount of slack, including with interest rate cuts. Alteratively, the BoE could simply be attempting to return inflation to target ona longer horizon than the 2-year interval shown.

title: Economics
text:
While my Economics training is at an early stage, I like to explore data to expand my understanding of the economy. Here, you will find several examples that combine macro with data science.
Monetary Policy
In 2017, BoE Governor Mark Carney gave a speech titled ‘Lambda’ describing how BoE policy had been confronted with a trade-off between returning inflation to the inflation target and stabilising output and how the Bank’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) confronted that trade-off.
In this note, I replicate and update parts of Governor Carney’s analysis. The period since Governor Carney’s speech has been marked by further episodes that imply a macro trade-off between reaching the inflation target and output stabilisation.
I draw on the fact that every three months since being made independent of the Government in 1997, the Bank of England has provided forecasts for how it expects the UK economy to evolve.

Data analysis of Lambda

Fiscal Policy
The UK faces an important fiscal challenge – to stabilise and bring down debt, provide good public services and support economic growth.
Since its creation in 2010, the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) has produced forecasts of the UK macro economy alongside each major fiscal event, including Budget.
In this Note, I provide a simple overview of UK fiscal policy

title: Leon
text:
I am a budding Economist. I am interested in how the economy is evolving and enjoy combining data and statistics as a way of exploring that evolution of the economy.
I am interested in data science to expand my knowledge of macroeconomics. I apply statistical analysis to explore my interest in sports analytics and am interested in design because I want to understand how to build things that look good and work well.
Here, you will find examples of my interest in each of these.

title: Sports analytics
text:
Sports analytics is revolutionising sport, including football. It affects every aspect of getting the best sporting performances from a team by using statistical analysis: recruiting the best players and selecting the best team for each match, achieving peak fitness as well as on-the-field tactics – without losing the magic of each unique and spontaneous football match.
Liverpool FC won the English Premiership in 2019/20 and in 2024/25, two of the greatest seasons ever.

title: UK: A Fiscal Overview
text:
Evolving views of the UK’s fiscal position
How the volume of UK public debt evolves over time reflects the accumulation of its borrowing decisions, when faced with economic shocks affecting the economy’s growth rate and interest rates on its debt.
The Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) has reported on the sustainability of UK public finances since it was formed in 2010. I draw on how official forecasts have evolved over time, even preceding the formation fo the OBR, to summarise how official HM Teasury and OBR views of the UK’s fiscal position have evolved - beginning with public debt and public borrowing.









Since the global financial crisis (GFC) in 2007/08, as trend UK growth weakened, the UK has faced a long-lasting fiscal challenge. This challenge has involved lowering the fiscal deficit to stabilise the public debt to GDP ratio. Since the Covid-19 pandemic and burst of inflation it has also been necessary to do that amid higher interest rates on debt.









Several of those features are illustrated in the following charts. I use the evolution fo official forecasts as a way of showing how views and expectations for these key features have evolved.


The required fiscal position has become more challenging
Public finance decisions are best summarised by looking at the evolution of the primary balance, which is annual public borrowing excluding interest payments. This is the measure of public borrowing that spending and tax decisions control, although those decisions need to take account of the interest rate environment, as I show below.
The primary balance required to stabilise the public debt to GDP ratio depends on the growth rate of the economy and the interest rate on public debt. The following table shows how the required primary balance to stabilise the public debt to GDP ratio has evolved for different combinations of growth and interest rates.
Required, Debt-stabilising, Primary Balance (% GDP).

Note: Calculations assume Debt/GDP at 100% and no stock-flow adjustments, for different combinations of 'r' and 'g'.








The key point from the table is that, since the financial crisis, a slowing in UK growth and a more recent rise in interest rates have made it more difficult to stabilise the public debt to GDP ratio – the required primary balance has increased.
The UK has shifted from the bottom/left part of this table – when the average nominal growth rate was around 4% and the effective nominal interest rate was around 2.5-3.0% towards the middle/right part of the table. While a primary deficit around 1.0-1.5% used to be sufficient to stabilise the public debt to GDP ratio, now an ongoing primary surplus around 1% of GDP is required.
That captures the sense in which the UK’s required fiscal position has become more challenging.


A dramatic increase in annual interest payments on the public debt
As the Debt ratio has increased over time and, more recently, interest rates have risen, the interest payments on the public debt have also increased. The following chart shows how the OBR’s forecasts for interest payments have shifted higher over time.
Whereas the UK used to expect annual interest payments around £40-50bn between 2010 and 2020, forecasts since 2022, and the onset of Covid-19 and the Russian invasion of Ukraine have seen these jump higher to over £100bn per year.











Fiscal choices at fiscal events
Public finance choices are made by the Chancellor at national fiscal events, including the annual Budget.
The chart below summarises different fiscal choices at each fiscal event – either to tighten or loosen fiscal policy – and relates those choices to the macro news affecting the fiscal position at that event.









The chart shows that fiscal choices have been made in response to macro news, with fiscal tightening more likely when the macro news is negative and loosening more likely when the macro news is positive. The result is a clear downward-sloped relationship between fiscal choices and macro news.
There is, however, significant variation in fiscal choices for a given level of macro news. For example, the first Budget by Chancellor Reeves in October 2024 saw significant fiscal loosening at around 1% of GDP, reflecting a large boost to public spending, only partly funded by increased taxes. There had been little fiscal news at the time.
The subsequent 2025 Spring Statement too back some of that fiscal loosening, with fiscal news at the time raising the borrowing forecast.
Overall, policy space has become more limited over time owing both to a more challenging fiscal environment and fiscal choices that have tended to postpone fiscal tightening. The UK has less room to respond to future fiscal shocks.

title: Design
text:
Design shapes how we experience the world. What Antony Gormley said about Art also applies to Design, it being “the means by which we communicate what it feels like to be alive.”
Here, I collect several of my favourite examples of art and design.
Buildings and Spaces
The Guggenheim museum, Bilbao, Spain.
Reina Sofia museum, Madrid, Spain.
The High Line, New York, USA.
The Alhambra, Granada, Spain.
Sculpture
Another Place, Crosby, Merseyside.
Golden Boy of Pye Corner, London.
El Oso y El Madrono, Madrid, Spain.
Clothes
Trainers by Adidas.
Psylo, Camden.

title: Data Analysis and the English 2019/20 Premier League Season
text: No statistical analysis can do justice to the excitement of the 2019/20 Premier League season. Without any coincidence, the season was won by Liverpool. Here, I analyse match data from every teams’ matches, capturing results, aspects of performance including both attack and defense, as well as discipline. Liverpool were unsurpassed in almost every dimension. On 99 points for the season, Liverpool, beat Manchester City by 18 points and the third and fourth placed teams by 33 points.

title: Data Analysis and the English 2019/20 Premier League Season

The following density plots capture, team-by-team, shot and save accuracy and how this varies. My impression is this does not vary so much across teams, including between the best and weakest teams. What matters more is the number of shot attempts and preventing an opposing team from shooting.
